refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve a measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target for the load→travel→dump cycle under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, with cycle time performance monitored and reported via telemetry.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-002 is a key performance indicator (KPI) requirement that directly impacts customer productivity and system acceptance. Refinement adds explicit reference to measurement/monitoring (telemetry) and site conditions to ensure testability and context-specificity. High safety criticality tailoring requires that cycle time optimization does not compromise safety interlocks or emergency response.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct field trials under representative duty cycle and site conditions; collect telemetry data for ≥100 cycles; compute p95 cycle time; compare against CT_target. Verify that safety-critical functions (e.g., load securing, emergency stop) are not bypassed or degraded in pursuit of cycle time.
sub_frs: FR-2.1: The NGHE solution shall measure and record cycle time for each load→travel→dump cycle via onboard instrumentation.
FR-2.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit cycle time telemetry to the site management system for real-time and historical analysis.
FR-2.3: The NGHE solution shall execute the load→travel→dump cycle within CT_target under nominal duty cycle and site conditions without compromising safety interlocks.
FR-2.4: The NGHE solution shall provide operator feedback (visual/auditory) on cycle time performance and any deviations from target.

candidate_dps: DP-2.1: Implement integrated cycle time measurement and optimization logic within the vehicle control system; use onboard sensors (position, speed, load) to detect cycle phase transitions and compute elapsed time; transmit telemetry via network link.

DP-2.2: Implement a distributed cycle time monitoring architecture: vehicle collects raw sensor data; teleoperation system aggregates and analyzes cycle time; site management system stores and reports KPI.

DP-2.3: Implement a hybrid approach: vehicle performs real-time cycle time measurement and safety validation; teleoperation system provides operator feedback and remote optimization recommendations; safety services validate that cycle time targets do not conflict with safety constraints.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-2.3: Implement a hybrid approach: vehicle performs real-time cycle time measurement and safety validation; teleoperation system provides operator feedback and remote optimization recommendations; safety services validate that cycle time targets do not conflict with safety constraints.

potential_sources: Coupling between vehicle cycle time measurement and safety services validation: vehicle must expose cycle time data and safety state to safety services for conflict detection.
Coupling between teleoperation feedback and vehicle control: operator recommendations must be validated against vehicle state and safety constraints before execution.
Coupling between telemetry transmission and network link availability: cycle time data must be buffered locally if network is unavailable.
coupling_check.notes: DP-2.3 distributes responsibility across three domains (vehicle, teleoperation, safety) to avoid monolithic control logic and enable independent optimization. Couplings are managed via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking). High safety criticality requires that safety services have veto authority over cycle time optimization.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle onboard sensors (position, speed, load) are sufficiently accurate and responsive to detect cycle phase transitions within ±0.5 sec.
Assumption: Network link has sufficient bandwidth and latency (≤500 ms round-trip) to support real-time operator feedback without degrading cycle time.
Risk: If safety services validation introduces latency >2 sec, cycle time optimization may be blocked or delayed, reducing effectiveness.
Risk: If teleoperation system is unavailable, vehicle must fall back to autonomous cycle time measurement and local safety validation; fallback logic must be pre-validated.
Risk: Operator recommendations for cycle time optimization may conflict with site safety plan or worksite policies; safety services must enforce policy compliance.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the decision authority and latency budget for cycle time optimization: (1) Define maximum acceptable latency for safety services validation (e.g., 1 sec). (2) Specify fallback behavior if teleoperation system is unavailable (autonomous mode with conservative cycle time targets). (3) Document site safety plan constraints that may limit cycle time optimization (e.g., speed limits in congested areas, mandatory pauses for load securing).

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: The Vehicle module owns real-time cycle time measurement, phase detection, and local safety validation. It collects sensor data (position, speed, load), computes cycle time for each phase, validates that cycle time targets do not violate safety interlocks, and transmits telemetry via the Network Link interface. The Vehicle module also implements fallback autonomous cycle time measurement if teleoperation is unavailable.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits cycle time data (phase durations, p95 estimates, safety state) to Teleoperation and site management system. Frequency: ≥1 Hz during active cycle; buffered if network unavailable.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives safety constraints and veto signals from Safety Services (e.g., 'do not exceed 5 m/s in load phase'). Vehicle transmits cycle time performance data and safety state to Safety Services for conflict detection.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives cycle time optimization recommendations from Teleoperation (e.g., 'increase travel speed to 8 m/s'). Vehicle validates recommendations against local safety state before execution.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle uses Network Link to transmit Telemetry and receive Tasking/SafetyAlert. Network Link provides buffering and retry logic for reliability.

module_containment_scores.notes: Vehicle is the natural owner of real-time cycle time measurement and phase detection because it has direct access to onboard sensors and control actuators. Cycle time measurement logic is tightly coupled to vehicle dynamics and safety interlocks. Diffusion risk is low: measurement logic is localized to vehicle control firmware. External interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking) are well-defined and minimal.

Teleoperation could own cycle time analysis and operator feedback, but should not own real-time measurement or safety validation. If Teleoperation owns measurement, it creates a distributed measurement architecture with latency and synchronization risks. Diffusion risk is high: cycle time logic would be split between vehicle and teleoperation, making it difficult to maintain consistency and validate safety constraints.

Safety Services owns validation that cycle time targets do not conflict with safety constraints, but should not own real-time measurement. If Safety Services owns measurement, it creates a dependency on external validation for every cycle, introducing latency and single-point-of-failure risk. Containment score reflects ownership of constraint validation logic, not measurement.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container, but assigning DP-2.3 to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across all sub-modules without clear ownership. Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the optimal owner of cycle time measurement and real-time safety validation because: (1) It has direct access to onboard sensors and control actuators required for accurate phase detection. (2) It can perform safety validation with minimal latency (<100 ms), critical for high-safety-criticality systems. (3) It can implement autonomous fallback if teleoperation is unavailable. (4) Measurement logic is tightly coupled to vehicle dynamics and does not naturally diffuse to other modules. Teleoperation and Safety Services are consumers of cycle time data and providers of optimization recommendations and safety constraints, respectively.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle firmware complexity increases with cycle time measurement and optimization logic; firmware validation and testing burden grows.
Risk: If vehicle onboard sensors are unreliable or drift over time, cycle time measurement accuracy degrades; sensor calibration and health monitoring must be implemented.
Risk: Vehicle must implement buffering and retry logic for telemetry transmission; if buffer overflows, cycle time data is lost and p95 estimates become inaccurate.
Risk: Vehicle must validate operator recommendations (Tasking) against local safety state; if validation logic is incorrect or incomplete, unsafe cycle time targets may be executed.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the scope of Vehicle ownership: (1) Vehicle owns real-time cycle time measurement, phase detection, and local safety validation. (2) Vehicle does NOT own site-level cycle time analysis, KPI reporting, or operator feedback UI; these are owned by Teleoperation and site management system. (3) Define the interface contract for Tasking: Vehicle must validate and reject recommendations that violate safety constraints; Teleoperation must handle rejection gracefully. (4) Implement sensor health monitoring and calibration logic in Vehicle to ensure measurement accuracy over time.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-2.3 (hybrid approach with vehicle measurement, teleoperation feedback, and safety services validation) directly addresses the refined FR requirement to achieve p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target while maintaining safety. The DP distributes responsibility appropriately across domains and includes explicit safety validation to address high safety criticality tailoring. No decoupling or independence violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Cycle time measurement logic is localized to Vehicle, but optimization recommendations flow through Teleoperation and SafetyAlert interfaces. If interface contracts are not clearly defined, diffusion of cycle time logic across modules may occur.
Moderate risk: Vehicle must implement buffering and retry logic for telemetry transmission; if this logic is not well-encapsulated, it may diffuse to other vehicle subsystems.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle containment score of 0.95 is strong. Measurement and safety validation logic is tightly coupled to vehicle control and does not naturally diffuse. External interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking) are well-defined and minimal. Ownership boundary is clear: Vehicle owns measurement and local validation; Teleoperation and Safety Services own analysis and constraint validation, respectively.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via field trials: (1) Instrument vehicle with cycle time measurement logic. (2) Collect telemetry data for ≥100 cycles under representative duty cycle and site conditions. (3) Compute p95 cycle time and compare against CT_target. (4) Verify that safety interlocks are not bypassed or degraded. (5) Validate that operator recommendations are correctly validated against safety constraints.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Vehicle owns measurement and local safety validation; Teleoperation owns operator feedback and optimization recommendations; Safety Services owns constraint validation. Interfaces are well-defined (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link). Ownership boundaries are distinct and non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Clarify the decision authority for cycle time optimization: Safety Services has veto authority; Teleoperation provides recommendations; Vehicle executes validated recommendations.
Define latency budgets for each interface: Telemetry transmission ≤500 ms; SafetyAlert validation ≤1 sec; Tasking recommendation execution ≤2 sec.
Specify fallback behavior if teleoperation is unavailable: Vehicle operates autonomously with conservative cycle time targets (e.g., 90% of CT_target).
Document sensor health monitoring and calibration requirements for Vehicle to ensure measurement accuracy over time.

decision.justification: The refined FR-2 (cycle time KPI) is not atomic and requires further decomposition into sub-FRs. The four sub-FRs (measurement, telemetry transmission, cycle execution within target, operator feedback) are distinct functional concerns that require separate DP and IM assignments. DP-2.3 is a high-level solution concept that spans multiple sub-FRs; decomposition is necessary to allocate responsibility and verify each sub-FR independently. Vehicle is the appropriate IM boundary for the measurement and safety validation sub-FRs, but teleoperation and safety services will own other sub-FRs.
decision.justification_detail: Decomposition is justified because: (1) FR-2.1 (measurement) and FR-2.3 (cycle execution) are tightly coupled to vehicle control and should be owned by Vehicle. (2) FR-2.2 (telemetry transmission) involves network and data management concerns that may be shared with other telemetry streams; decomposition allows independent optimization. (3) FR-2.4 (operator feedback) is a user interface concern that should be owned by Teleoperation, not Vehicle. (4) High safety criticality requires explicit decomposition of safety validation logic (FR-2.3) to ensure it is not overlooked or diffused.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-2.1: The NGHE solution shall measure and record cycle time for each load→travel→dump cycle via onboard instrumentation.
FR-2.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit cycle time telemetry to the site management system for real-time and historical analysis.
FR-2.3: The NGHE solution shall execute the load→travel→dump cycle within CT_target under nominal duty cycle and site conditions without compromising safety interlocks.
FR-2.4: The NGHE solution shall provide operator feedback (visual/auditory) on cycle time performance and any deviations from target.

assumptions: Vehicle onboard sensors (position, speed, load) are sufficiently accurate and responsive to detect cycle phase transitions within ±0.5 sec.
Network link has sufficient bandwidth and latency (≤500 ms round-trip) to support real-time operator feedback without degrading cycle time.
Site safety plan and operational policies do not impose constraints that make CT_target unachievable (e.g., mandatory pauses that exceed CT_target).
Operator recommendations for cycle time optimization are provided via Tasking interface with sufficient lead time (≥2 sec) to allow vehicle validation and execution.
Safety Services validation latency is acceptable (≤1 sec) and does not block cycle time optimization.
open_risks: Risk: If vehicle onboard sensors are unreliable or drift over time, cycle time measurement accuracy degrades; sensor calibration and health monitoring must be implemented.
Risk: If network link is unavailable or has high latency, teleoperation feedback is delayed and cycle time optimization is blocked; fallback autonomous mode must be pre-validated.
Risk: If Safety Services validation introduces latency >2 sec, cycle time optimization may be blocked or delayed, reducing effectiveness.
Risk: If operator recommendations conflict with site safety plan or worksite policies, Safety Services must enforce policy compliance; policy enforcement logic must be pre-defined and validated.

Risk: If vehicle firmware complexity increases significantly with cycle time measurement and optimization logic, firmware validation and testing burden grows; risk mitigation requires modular firmware architecture and comprehensive testing.
evidence_needed: Field trial data: ≥100 cycles under representative duty cycle and site conditions; measured p95 cycle time; comparison against CT_target.
Sensor accuracy and responsiveness data: sensor calibration certificates; sensor drift analysis over time; phase transition detection latency measurements.
Network link performance data: bandwidth utilization; latency measurements; packet loss rates; buffer overflow events.
Safety Services validation latency data: validation decision time; policy enforcement success rate; conflict detection accuracy.
Operator feedback effectiveness data: operator acceptance of recommendations; recommendation execution success rate; cycle time improvement achieved.
Fallback autonomous mode validation: autonomous cycle time measurement accuracy; conservative cycle time target selection; safety constraint enforcement in autonomous mode.
